title: The Mostert–Shields Theorems — Classification of Continuous T-Norms and T-Conorms

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the three canonical t-norms $F^1_{\wedge}$, $F^2_{\wedge}$, $F^3_{\wedge}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F^1_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$, corresponding to Gödel / $\mathbb{F}^1$ logic.
$F^2_{\wedge}(x,y) = xy$, corresponding to Product / $\mathbb{F}^2$ logic.
$F^3_{\wedge}(x,y) = \max(0, x+y-1)$, corresponding to Łukasiewicz / $\mathbb{F}^3$ logic.
$F^2_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$ and $F^1_{\wedge}(x,y) = xy$.
The three canonical t-norms are mutually isomorphic as ordered structures.

explanations.text: $F^1_\wedge(x,y) = \min(x,y)$ is the Gödel/minimum t-norm.
explanations.notes_quote: $F^1_\wedge(x,y)$: $\min(x,y)$, Godel / $\mathbb{F}^1$

explanations.text: $F^2_\wedge(x,y) = xy$ is the product t-norm.
explanations.notes_quote: $F^2_\wedge(x,y)$: $xy$, Product / $\mathbb{F}^2$

explanations.text: $F^3_\wedge(x,y) = \max(0,x+y-1)$ is the Łukasiewicz t-norm.
explanations.notes_quote: $F^3_\wedge(x,y)$: $\max(0, x+y-1)$, Łukasiewicz / $\mathbb{F}^3$

explanations.text: The labels are swapped. $F^1_\wedge = \min$ and $F^2_\wedge = xy$, not the other way around.
The three canonical t-norms are NOT mutually isomorphic. The notes explicitly state this: $F^1_\wedge(x,x) = x$ for all $x$, whereas $F^2_\wedge(x,x) = x^2 < x$ for $0 < x < 1$.
explanations.notes_quote: These are not mutually isomorphic: $F^1_\wedge(x,x) = x$ for all $x$, whereas $F^2_\wedge(x,x) = x^2 < x$ for $0 < x < 1$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the disc notation $B_{\delta}(a,b)$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $B_{\delta}(a,b)$ is the open disc of radius $\delta$ centred at $(a,b)$ in $[0,1]^2$.
$B_{\delta}(a,b)$ is used in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ formulation of continuity for functions of two variables.
$B_{\delta}(a,b) = \{(x,y) \in [0,1]^2 : |x-a| < \delta \text{ and } |y-b| < \delta\}$.
Topologists call $B_{\delta}(a,b)$ a 'ball' of dimension 2.

explanations.text: This is the exact definition of $B_\delta(a,b)$ given in the notes.
The notes describe $B_\delta(a,b)$ as an open disc of radius $\delta$ centred at $(a,b)$.
explanations.notes_quote: the open disc of radius $\delta$ centred at $(a,b) \in [0,1]^2$

explanations.text: The disc notation is introduced specifically for the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ formulation of continuity for binary functions.
explanations.notes_quote: This is used in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ formulation of continuity for functions of two variables

explanations.text: This is the definition of a square (Chebyshev ball), not the Euclidean open disc. The disc uses the Euclidean distance $(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 < \delta^2$.
The notes parenthetically note that topologists call this a 'ball' of dimension 2.
explanations.notes_quote: Topologists call this a 'ball' of dimension 2, hence the notation $B$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state axioms (C1)–(C4) for a continuous t-norm $\wedge : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: (C1) Boolean: $0 \wedge 0 = 1 \wedge 0 = 0 \wedge 1 = 0$ and $1 \wedge 1 = 1$.
(C2) Increasing: if $z < z'$ then $(x \wedge z) \leq (x \wedge z')$ and $(z \wedge y) \leq (z' \wedge y)$.
(C3) Continuous: for all $(a,b) \in [0,1]^2$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, $\exists \delta > 0$ such that $|(x \wedge y) - (x' \wedge y')| < \varepsilon$ whenever $(x,y) \in B_{\delta}(a,b)$.
(C4) Commutative: $x \wedge y = y \wedge x$ for all $x, y \in [0,1]$.
(C4) Associative: $(x \wedge y) \wedge z = x \wedge (y \wedge z)$.

explanations.text: (C1) specifies the Boolean boundary behaviour of the t-norm.
explanations.notes_quote: (C1) Boolean: $0 \wedge 0 = 1 \wedge 0 = 0 \wedge 1 = 0$ and $1 \wedge 1 = 1$

explanations.text: (C2) requires $\wedge$ to be monotone (increasing) in each argument.
explanations.notes_quote: (C2) Increasing (monotone): $\wedge$ is increasing in each component. That is, if $z < z'$ then $(x \wedge z) \leq (x \wedge z')$ and $(z \wedge y) \leq (z' \wedge y)$

explanations.text: (C3) is the epsilon-delta continuity condition for the binary function $\wedge$.
explanations.notes_quote: (C3) Continuous: $\wedge$ is continuous as a function $[0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$

explanations.text: Commutativity is NOT one of the four axioms (C1)–(C4). The four axioms are: (C1) Boolean, (C2) Increasing, (C3) Continuous, (C4) Associative.
(C4) is associativity of $\wedge$.
explanations.notes_quote: (C4) Associative: $((x \wedge y) \wedge z) = (x \wedge (y \wedge z))$

questions.text: Which of the following are derived properties of any t-norm (provable from (C1)–(C4))? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $x \wedge 0 = 0 \wedge x = 0$ for all $x \in [0,1]$.
$x \wedge 1 = 1 \wedge x = x$ for all $x \in [0,1]$.
$x \wedge x \leq x$ for all $x \in [0,1]$.
$x \wedge x = x$ for all $x \in [0,1]$ (idempotency).
$x \wedge y = y \wedge x$ for all $x,y$ (commutativity is derived, not axiomatic).

explanations.text: $x \wedge 0 = 0$ is a derived property (i) from (C1)–(C4).
explanations.notes_quote: (i) $x \wedge 0 = 0 \wedge x = 0$

explanations.text: $x \wedge 1 = x$ is derived property (ii).
explanations.notes_quote: (ii) $x \wedge 1 = 1 \wedge x = x$

explanations.text: $x \wedge x \leq x$ is derived property (iii).
explanations.notes_quote: (iii) $x \wedge x \leq x$

explanations.text: Idempotency ($x \wedge x = x$) does NOT hold for all t-norms. For $F^2_\wedge$: $x \wedge x = x^2 < x$ for $x \in (0,1)$. The correct derived property is $x \wedge x \leq x$.
Commutativity is not listed as a derived property in the notes; it is not one of the four axioms (C1)–(C4) either. The notes do not claim commutativity follows from (C1)–(C4) in general.

questions.text: Regarding the result that continuity implies limit-commutativity, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $\wedge$ satisfies (C3), then $\left(\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n\right) \wedge y = \lim_{n \to \infty}(x_n \wedge y)$ for any convergent sequence $x_n$ and any $y$.
The proof uses: given $\varepsilon > 0$, by (C3) there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $(x_n, y) \in B_\delta(L, y)$ implies $|(x_n \wedge y) - (L \wedge y)| < \varepsilon$.
The result requires $\wedge$ to satisfy all four axioms (C1)–(C4), not just continuity.
Since $x_n \to L$, there exists $N$ such that $|x_n - L| < \delta$ for $n \geq N$, placing $(x_n,y)$ inside $B_\delta(L,y)$.
This result shows that limits and $\wedge$ can always be interchanged, regardless of continuity.

explanations.text: This is the exact statement of the limit-commutativity result.
explanations.notes_quote: $\left(\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n\right) \wedge y = \lim_{n \to \infty}(x_n \wedge y)$

explanations.text: The proof proceeds exactly this way using (C3).
explanations.notes_quote: by (C3) there is some $\delta > 0$ such that $|(x_n \wedge y) - (L \wedge y)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $(x_n, y) \in B_\delta(L, y)$

explanations.text: The proof only uses (C3) (continuity). The other axioms (C1), (C2), (C4) are not required for this particular result.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\wedge$ satisfies (C3), then...

explanations.text: This is the key step linking the sequence convergence to the disc condition.
explanations.notes_quote: Since $x_n \to L$, there is $N \geq 0$ such that $|x_n - L| < \delta$ for all $n \geq N$, which places $(x_n, y)$ inside $B_\delta(L,y)$

explanations.text: The interchange of limits with $\wedge$ requires continuity (C3). Without continuity, it need not hold.

questions.text: In the verification that $F^1_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$ is a continuous t-norm, which of the following steps are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: (C4): $\min(\min(x,y),z) = \min(x,\min(y,z))$ because $t \leq \min(a,b) \iff t \leq a$ and $t \leq b$.
(C3): When $a = b$, take $\delta = \varepsilon$; when $a \neq b$, choose $\delta = \min(\varepsilon, |a-b|/(2\sqrt{2}))$ to keep the disc in one half-plane.
(C2) for $\min$: if $z < z'$ and $x \leq z$, then $\min(x,z) = x = \min(x,z')$.
(C3): The key difficulty arises at points where $a = b$, where a larger $\delta$ must be used.

explanations.text: Direct computation verifies (C1) for $\min$.
Associativity of min follows from: $t \leq \min(\min(x,y),z)$ iff $t \leq x$ and $t \leq y$ and $t \leq z$ iff $t \leq \min(x,\min(y,z))$.
explanations.notes_quote: $t \leq \min(\min(x,y),z) \iff t \leq x$ and $t \leq y$ and $t \leq z \iff t \leq \min(x,\min(y,z))$

explanations.text: The continuity proof splits into cases $a = b$ and $a \neq b$ with different $\delta$ choices.
explanations.notes_quote: If $a = b$: take $\delta = \varepsilon$... If $a \neq b$: choose $\delta = \min\!\left(\varepsilon, \frac{|a-b|}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)$

explanations.text: This is one of the three cases for verifying (C2) for $\min$.
explanations.notes_quote: If $x \leq z$: $\min(x,z) = x \leq x = \min(x,z')$

explanations.text: The case $a = b$ is actually simpler ($\delta = \varepsilon$ suffices). The harder case is $a \neq b$, which requires keeping the disc in a single half-plane.

questions.text: In the verification of (C3) for $F^2_{\wedge}(x,y) = xy$, with $x = a+u$, $y = b+v$ and $|u|,|v| < \delta$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $|xy - ab| = |(a+u)(b+v) - ab| = |av + bu + uv|$.
$|av + bu + uv| \leq a|v| + b|u| + |uv| < \delta + \delta + \delta^2$ (using $a,b \leq 1$ and $|u|,|v| < \delta$).
Choosing $\delta = \varepsilon/3$ ensures $\delta + \delta + \delta^2 \leq \varepsilon$.
Choosing $\delta = \varepsilon/2$ ensures $\delta + \delta + \delta^2 \leq \varepsilon$.
(C4) for $F^2_\wedge = xy$: $(xy)z = x(yz)$ by associativity of multiplication over $\mathbb{R}$.

explanations.text: Expanding $(a+u)(b+v) - ab = av + bu + uv$.
explanations.notes_quote: $|xy - ab| = |(a+u)(b+v) - ab| = |av + bu + uv|$

explanations.text: Triangle inequality and $a,b \leq 1$ give the bound $\delta + \delta + \delta^2$.
explanations.notes_quote: $|av + bu + uv| \leq a|v| + b|u| + |uv| < \delta + \delta + \delta^2$

explanations.text: $\delta = \varepsilon/3$: $\delta + \delta + \delta^2 = \varepsilon/3 + \varepsilon/3 + (\varepsilon/3)^2 \leq \varepsilon$ for small $\varepsilon$.
explanations.notes_quote: Choose $\delta = \varepsilon/3$; then $\delta + \delta + \delta^2 = \varepsilon/3 + \varepsilon/3 + (\varepsilon/3)^2 \leq \varepsilon$

explanations.text: $\delta = \varepsilon/2$: $\varepsilon/2 + \varepsilon/2 + (\varepsilon/2)^2 = \varepsilon + \varepsilon^2/4 > \varepsilon$. The correct choice is $\delta = \varepsilon/3$.
Associativity of $xy$ over $\mathbb{R}$ immediately gives (C4).
explanations.notes_quote: (C4): $(xy)z = x(yz)$ by associativity of multiplication over $\mathbb{R}$

questions.source_ref: Section 3.3: F3 = max(0, x+y-1) — Verifying (C1) and (C2)
questions.text: In verifying that $F^3_{\wedge}(x,y) = \max(0, x+y-1)$ is a continuous t-norm, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: (C2): If $z < z'$, then $x+z-1 \leq x+z'-1$, so $\max(0,x+z-1) \leq \max(0,x+z'-1)$.
(C3): When $a+b < 1$, both $\max(0,x+y-1)$ and $\max(0,a+b-1)$ equal 0 for $(x,y) \in B_\delta(a,b)$ (small $\delta$), giving difference 0.
(C4) for $F^3_\wedge$ is verified by direct computation analogous to the associativity of bounded addition.

explanations.text: Direct computation verifies (C1) for the Łukasiewicz t-norm.
Since $x+z-1 \leq x+z'-1$ and both are clamped to $\geq 0$, the inequality holds.
explanations.notes_quote: $\max(0, x+z-1) \leq \max(0, x+z'-1)$ since $x + z - 1 \leq x + z' - 1$ and both are clamped to be $\geq 0$

explanations.text: When $a+b < 1$: choosing $\delta$ small enough keeps $x+y-1 < 0$ for $(x,y) \in B_\delta(a,b)$, so both outputs are 0.
explanations.notes_quote: If $(a,b)$ has $a+b < 1$: then $a+b-1 < 0$ and $x+y-1 < 0$ for $(x,y) \in B_\delta(a,b)$, so both sides are 0

explanations.text: $\max(0,1+1-1) = \max(0,1) = 1$, not 0. So $1 \wedge 1 = 1$ as required by (C1).
(C4) for $F^3_\wedge$ is verified by direct computation, similar to associativity of bounded addition.
explanations.notes_quote: (C4): Verified by direct computation (analogous to the associativity of bounded addition)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the 'chimera' concept in the Mostert–Shields Theorem? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Any continuous t-norm must be a chimera: it behaves like $F^1_\wedge$, $F^2_\wedge$, or $F^3_\wedge$ on each sub-interval of $[0,1]$.
A chimera t-norm behaves like $F^1_\wedge = \min$ OUTSIDE the sub-intervals and like $F^2_\wedge$ or $F^3_\wedge$ on each sub-interval.
$F^1_\wedge$, $F^2_\wedge$, $F^3_\wedge$ are the ONLY continuous t-norms (up to isomorphism).
The Mostert–Shields Theorem was published in 1957.
A chimera t-norm may include both type 2 (product-like) and type 3 (Łukasiewicz-like) sub-intervals.

explanations.text: Any continuous t-norm must be a chimera of the three canonical t-norms.
explanations.notes_quote: any continuous t-norm must be a chimera of these three, meaning it behaves like one of the three t-norms on each sub-interval of $[0,1]$

explanations.text: Outside the identified sub-intervals, the chimera behaves exactly like $\min$.
explanations.notes_quote: like $F^1_\wedge = \min$ outside those sub-intervals

explanations.text: This is FALSE. The notes state 'the answer is NO' — there are infinitely many continuous t-norms (all chimeras), not just three.
explanations.notes_quote: The answer is no — but any continuous t-norm must be a chimera of these three

explanations.text: Theorem 6.1 is attributed to Mostert–Shields (1957).
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem 6.1 (Mostert–Shields, 1957)

explanations.text: A chimera can contain multiple sub-intervals of both types (type 2 and type 3), each behaving differently.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly characterise the idempotent set $A = \{z \in [0,1] : z \wedge z = z\}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Always $0, 1 \in A$ for any t-norm, by axiom (C1).
For $F^1_\wedge = \min$: every $z \in [0,1]$ is in $A$.
For $F^2_\wedge = xy$: only $z = 0$ and $z = 1$ are in $A$.
For $F^3_\wedge = \max(0,x+y-1)$: only $\{0,1\}$ are in $A$.
For $F^2_\wedge = xy$: $z \in A$ iff $z^2 = z$, which holds for all $z \in [0,1]$.

explanations.text: By (C1): $0 \wedge 0 = 0$ and $1 \wedge 1 = 1$, so $0, 1 \in A$ always.
explanations.notes_quote: always $0, 1 \in A$ since $0 \wedge 0 = 0$ and $1 \wedge 1 = 1$ by (C1)

explanations.text: For $F^1_\wedge$: $\min(z,z) = z$ for every $z$, so $A = [0,1]$.
explanations.notes_quote: For $F^1_\wedge$, every $z \in [0,1]$ is in $A$

explanations.text: For $F^2_\wedge$: $z^2 = z$ iff $z = 0$ or $z = 1$.
explanations.notes_quote: For $F^2_\wedge$, only $z = 0$ and $z = 1$ are in $A$ (since $z^2 = z$ iff $z = 0$ or $z = 1$)

explanations.text: For $F^3_\wedge$: $\max(0,2z-1) = z$ requires $2z-1 = z$, i.e. $z=1$, or $0 = z$. So $A = \{0,1\}$.
explanations.notes_quote: For $F^3_\wedge$, only $\{0, 1\}$

explanations.text: $z^2 = z$ iff $z(z-1) = 0$ iff $z = 0$ or $z = 1$. It does NOT hold for all $z \in [0,1]$.

questions.source_ref: Section 4.3: Lemma 6.2 — Min Behaviour Near Idempotents
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state Lemma 6.2 and its proof? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Lemma 6.2: If $z \wedge z = z$ and $x \leq z \leq y$, then $x \wedge y = x = \min(x,y)$.
Lemma 6.2: If $z \wedge z = z$ and $y \leq z \leq x$, then $x \wedge y = y = \min(x,y)$.
The proof uses: $x = z \wedge t$ for some $t$ (by IVT), then $x = (z \wedge z) \wedge t = z \wedge (z \wedge t) \leq y \wedge (z \wedge t) = y \wedge x$.
Lemma 6.2 shows that outside the sub-intervals $[a_i, b_i]$ (where all points are idempotents), $\wedge$ must act like $\min$.
The proof of Lemma 6.2 uses only axioms (C1) and (C4), not (C2).

explanations.text: Part (i) of Lemma 6.2.
explanations.notes_quote: (i) For all $x \leq z \leq y$ we have $(x \wedge y) = x = \min(x,y)$

explanations.text: Part (ii) of Lemma 6.2.
explanations.notes_quote: (ii) For all $y \leq z \leq x$ we have $(x \wedge y) = y = \min(x,y)$

explanations.text: This is exactly the proof chain given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: $x = z \wedge t = (z \wedge z) \wedge t = z \wedge (z \wedge t) \leq y \wedge (z \wedge t) = y \wedge x$

explanations.text: Lemma 6.2 is the key to establishing that $\wedge = \min$ outside the sub-intervals identified by $A$.
explanations.notes_quote: Combine Lemma 6.2 (outside the sub-intervals, $\wedge$ equals $\min$)

explanations.text: The proof uses (C2) in the step $z \wedge (z \wedge t) \leq y \wedge (z \wedge t)$ (since $z \leq y$ and $\wedge$ is increasing). So (C2) is used.
explanations.notes_quote: $\leq y \wedge (z \wedge t)$ by (C2), since $z \leq y$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish Type 2 and Type 3 sub-intervals in the Mostert–Shields framework? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Both Type 2 and Type 3 sub-intervals $[a,b]$ satisfy: $a,b \in A$ and $(a,b) \cap A = \varnothing$.
Type 2: $z \wedge z \neq a$ for all $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^2_\wedge = xy$.
Type 3: $z \wedge z = a$ for some $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^3_\wedge = \max(0,x+y-1)$.
Type 2: $z \wedge z = b$ for some $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^2_\wedge$.
Type 3: $z \wedge z = a$ for some $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^1_\wedge = \min$.

explanations.text: Both types share the same framework: $a,b \in A$ with no idempotents in the interior $(a,b)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $a_i, b_i \in A$ and $(a_i, b_i) \cap A = \varnothing$

explanations.text: Type 2 intervals are those where $z \wedge z \neq a$ for all interior $z$, and they are isomorphic to $F^2_\wedge$.
explanations.notes_quote: Type 2: $z \wedge z \neq a_i$ for all $z \in (a_i, b_i)$ — behaves like $F^2_\wedge = xy$

explanations.text: Type 3 intervals are those where some interior $z$ satisfies $z \wedge z = a$, and they are isomorphic to $F^3_\wedge$.
explanations.notes_quote: Type 3: $z \wedge z = a_i$ for some $z \in (a_i, b_i)$ — behaves like $F^3_\wedge = \max(0, x+y-1)$

explanations.text: Type 2 is defined by $z \wedge z \neq a$ (NOT $= b$). The condition $z \wedge z = b$ is not how Type 2 is defined.
Type 3 intervals are isomorphic to $F^3_\wedge$ (Łukasiewicz), not $F^1_\wedge = \min$.
explanations.notes_quote: Type 3: ... behaves like $F^3_\wedge = \max(0, x+y-1)$

questions.source_ref: Section 4.5: Lemma 6.3 — Type 2 Intervals are Isomorphic to F2
questions.text: Regarding Lemma 6.3 (Type 2 intervals isomorphic to $F^2_{\wedge}$), which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The isomorphism $\rho : [0,1] \to [a,b]$ is defined using $\wedge$-exponentiation: $\rho(x) = c^{\wedge r}$ where $r = -\log_2(x)$.
$\wedge$-exponentiation satisfies $(x^{\wedge r} \wedge x^{\wedge s}) = x^{\wedge(r+s)}$ for real $r,s > 0$.
If $r < s$ then $x^{\wedge r} \geq x^{\wedge s}$ — exponentiation is order-reversing in the exponent.
The isomorphism $\rho$ maps $x = 0 \mapsto b$ and $x = 1 \mapsto a$.
Lemma 6.3 proves that $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, \times \rangle$.

explanations.text: The isomorphism uses $\wedge$-exponentiation with $r = -\log_2(x)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\rho(x) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } x = 0 \\ b & \text{if } x = 1 \\ c^{\wedge r} & \text{if } 0 < x < 1 \text{ where } r = -\log_2(x) \end{cases}$

explanations.text: The key algebraic property of $\wedge$-exponentiation.
explanations.notes_quote: $(x^{\wedge r} \wedge x^{\wedge s}) = x^{\wedge(r+s)}$ for real $r, s > 0$

explanations.text: Larger exponents give smaller values (since $x \wedge x \leq x$), so exponentiation is order-reversing.
explanations.notes_quote: If $r < s$ then $x^{\wedge r} \geq x^{\wedge s}$ (exponentiation is order-reversing)

explanations.text: The isomorphism maps $x=0 \mapsto a$ and $x=1 \mapsto b$, not the other way round.
explanations.notes_quote: $\rho(x) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } x = 0 \\ b & \text{if } x = 1 \end{cases}$

explanations.text: Lemma 6.3 states $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, \times \rangle$ for type 2 intervals.
explanations.notes_quote: $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, \times \rangle$

questions.source_ref: Section 4.6: Lemma 6.4 — Type 3 Intervals are Isomorphic to F3
questions.text: Regarding Lemma 6.4 (Type 3 intervals isomorphic to $F^3_{\wedge}$), which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The order-reversing map $\rho : [0,2] \to [a,b]$ is defined by $\rho(x) = c^{\wedge x}$ for $0 < x < 2$, with $\rho(0) = b$ and $\rho(2) = a$.
To get an order-preserving isomorphism, compose with $\sigma(x) = 2(1-x)$, giving $\rho \circ \sigma : [0,1] \to [a,b]$.
In a type 3 interval, the largest value $c \in [a,b]$ with $c \wedge c = a$ plays a key role in constructing the isomorphism.
For type 3, $c^{\wedge r} = a$ for all $r \geq 2$ (since $c \wedge c = a$ is an idempotent).
Lemma 6.4 proves $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^2_\wedge \rangle$ for type 3 intervals.

explanations.text: The order-reversing map $\rho$ from $[0,2]$ to $[a,b]$ is defined as stated.
explanations.notes_quote: $\rho(x) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } x = 2 \\ b & \text{if } x = 0 \\ c^{\wedge x} & \text{if } 0 < x < 2 \end{cases}$

explanations.text: Composing with the order-reversing $\sigma(x) = 2(1-x)$ yields an order-preserving isomorphism.
explanations.notes_quote: compose with the order-reversing isomorphism $\sigma : [0,1] \to [0,2]$ defined by $\sigma(x) = 2(1-x)$. Then $\rho \circ \sigma : [0,1] \to [a,b]$ is an order-preserving isomorphism

explanations.text: The largest $c$ in $[a,b]$ with $c \wedge c = a$ is used to define the $\wedge$-exponentiation base.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $c \in [a,b]$ be the largest value with $c \wedge c = a$

explanations.text: Since $c \wedge c = a$ and $a$ is an idempotent ($a \wedge a = a$), all further exponents give $a$.
explanations.notes_quote: $c^{\wedge r} = a$ for all $r \geq 2$ (since $c \wedge c = a$, which is an idempotent)

explanations.text: Lemma 6.4 proves $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^3_\wedge \rangle$ (Łukasiewicz), NOT $F^2_\wedge$ (product). Type 3 corresponds to $F^3_\wedge$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], \max(0, x+y-1), < \rangle$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state Theorem 6.1 (Mostert–Shields for $\wedge$)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $z \wedge z \neq a$ for all $z \in (a,b)$ (Type 2), then $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^2_\wedge \rangle$.
If $z \wedge z = a$ for some $z \in (a,b)$ (Type 3), then $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^3_\wedge \rangle$.
Outside the sub-intervals (when $x,y$ do not share any common sub-interval $[a,b]$ with $a,b \in A$, $(a,b) \cap A = \varnothing$): $(x \wedge y) = \min(x,y)$.
The theorem is proved by combining Lemmas 6.2, 6.3, and 6.4.
Outside sub-intervals, $\wedge$ behaves like $F^2_\wedge = xy$.

explanations.text: Type 2 case of Theorem 6.1.
explanations.notes_quote: If $z \wedge z \neq a$ for all $z \in (a,b)$ (Type 2) then $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^2_\wedge \rangle$

explanations.text: Type 3 case of Theorem 6.1.
explanations.notes_quote: If $z \wedge z = a$ for some $z \in (a,b)$ (Type 3) then $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^3_\wedge \rangle$

explanations.text: Outside the sub-intervals, the t-norm is exactly $\min$.
explanations.notes_quote: Otherwise... $(x \wedge y) = F^1_\wedge(x,y) = \min(x,y)$

explanations.text: The proof summary cites exactly these three lemmas.
explanations.notes_quote: Combine Lemma 6.2 (outside the sub-intervals, $\wedge$ equals $\min$) with Lemma 6.3 (type 2 sub-intervals are isomorphic to $F^2_\wedge$) and Lemma 6.4 (type 3 sub-intervals are isomorphic to $F^3_\wedge$)

explanations.text: Outside sub-intervals, $\wedge$ behaves like $F^1_\wedge = \min$, NOT $F^2_\wedge = xy$.
explanations.notes_quote: $(x \wedge y) = F^1_\wedge(x,y) = \min(x,y)$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the axioms for a continuous t-conorm $\vee : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: (D1) Boolean: $0 \vee 0 = 0$ and $1 \vee 0 = 0 \vee 1 = 1 \vee 1 = 1$.
(D2) Increasing: if $z < z'$ then $(x \vee z) \leq (x \vee z')$ and $(z \vee y) \leq (z' \vee y)$.
(D3) Continuous: for all $(a,b) \in [0,1]^2$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, $\exists \delta > 0$ such that $|(x \vee y) - (a \vee b)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $(x,y) \in B_\delta(a,b)$.
(D4) Associative: $(x \vee y) \vee z = x \vee (y \vee z)$.
(D1) Boolean: $1 \vee 1 = 0$ and $0 \vee 0 = 1$.

explanations.text: (D1) is the Boolean boundary condition for t-conorms.
explanations.notes_quote: (D1) Boolean: $0 \vee 0 = 0$ and $1 \vee 0 = 0 \vee 1 = 1 \vee 1 = 1$

explanations.text: (D2) is the monotonicity condition for t-conorms.
explanations.notes_quote: (D2) Increasing: If $z < z'$ then $(x \vee z) \leq (x \vee z')$ and $(z \vee y) \leq (z' \vee y)$

explanations.text: (D3) is continuity in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ sense for binary functions.
explanations.notes_quote: (D3) Continuous: For all $(a,b) \in [0,1]^2$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, there is $\delta > 0$ such that $|(x \vee y) - (a \vee b)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $(x,y) \in B_\delta(a,b)$

explanations.text: (D4) is associativity of $\vee$.
explanations.notes_quote: (D4) Associative: $((x \vee y) \vee z) = (x \vee (y \vee z))$

explanations.text: This reverses (D1). For a t-conorm: $0 \vee 0 = 0$ (not 1) and $1 \vee 1 = 1$ (not 0). The pattern is dual to t-norms.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state Theorem 6.6 (Mostert–Shields for $\vee$)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Let $A = \{z \in [0,1] : z \vee z = z\}$. For $[a,b]$ with $a,b \in A$ and $(a,b) \cap A = \varnothing$:
If $z \vee z \neq b$ for all $z \in (a,b)$: $\langle [a,b], <, \vee \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^2_\vee \rangle$.
If $z \vee z = b$ for some $z \in (a,b)$: $\langle [a,b], <, \vee \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^3_\vee \rangle$.
Otherwise: $(x \vee y) = F^1_\vee(x,y) = \max(x,y)$.
For t-conorms, type 2 intervals are those where $z \vee z = a$ for some $z \in (a,b)$.

explanations.text: The idempotent set for t-conorms is $A = \{z : z \vee z = z\}$.
explanations.notes_quote: let $A = \{z \in [0,1] : z \vee z = z\}$

explanations.text: If no interior $z$ satisfies $z \vee z = b$, the interval is isomorphic to $F^2_\vee$.
explanations.notes_quote: If $z \vee z \neq b$ for all $z \in (a,b)$: $\langle [a,b], <, \vee \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^2_\vee \rangle$

explanations.text: If some interior $z$ satisfies $z \vee z = b$, the interval is isomorphic to $F^3_\vee$.
explanations.notes_quote: If $z \vee z = b$ for some $z \in (a,b)$: $\langle [a,b], <, \vee \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^3_\vee \rangle$

explanations.text: Outside the sub-intervals, $\vee$ equals $\max$.
explanations.notes_quote: Otherwise: $(x \vee y) = F^1_\vee(x,y) = \max(x,y)$

explanations.text: For t-conorms, the type 2 condition is $z \vee z \neq b$ (not $= a$). The $b$ is the upper endpoint, reflecting the dual structure.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.3: Deriving the Disjunction Result from Conjunction
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe how the Mostert–Shields theorem for $\vee$ is derived from the theorem for $\wedge$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For any t-conorm $\vee$ satisfying (D1)–(D4), define $x \wedge y = 1 - ((1-x) \vee (1-y))$; this $\wedge$ satisfies (C1)–(C4).
If $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^k_\wedge \rangle$ then $\langle [a,b], <, \vee \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^k_\vee \rangle$.
The three canonical t-conorms are $F^1_\vee = \max(x,y)$, $F^2_\vee = x+y-xy$, $F^3_\vee = \min(1,x+y)$.
The duality relation is $x \vee y = 1 - ((1-x) \wedge (1-y))$, derived by applying De Morgan's law.

explanations.text: The duality construction: from any t-conorm, one can define a t-norm via this formula.
explanations.notes_quote: For any t-conorm $\vee$ satisfying (D1)–(D4), define $(x \wedge y) = 1 - ((1-x) \vee (1-y))$. This $\wedge$ satisfies (C1)–(C4)

explanations.text: The three pairs of canonical t-norms and t-conorms are related by this duality.
The isomorphism type transfers from $\wedge$ to $\vee$ via the duality.
explanations.notes_quote: if $\langle [a,b], <, \wedge \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^k_\wedge \rangle$ for $k = 2, 3$, then $\langle [a,b], <, \vee \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, F^k_\vee \rangle$

explanations.text: The three canonical t-conorms are $\max$, $x+y-xy$, and $\min(1,x+y)$.
The duality formula $x \vee y = 1 - ((1-x) \wedge (1-y))$ is equivalent to De Morgan's law.

questions.source_ref: Section 6: Question 7.4 — Continuity Implies Limit Commutativity
questions.text: In the solution to Question 7.4 (continuity implies limit-commutativity), which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Let $L = \lim x_n$. We want to show $\lim_{n\to\infty}(x_n \wedge y) = L \wedge y$.
By (C3), for any $\varepsilon > 0$, $\exists \delta > 0$ such that $(x_n, y) \in B_\delta(L,y)$ implies $|(x_n \wedge y) - (L \wedge y)| < \varepsilon$.
Since $x_n \to L$, $\exists N$ such that $|x_n - L| < \delta$ for $n \geq N$, which means $(x_n,y) \in B_\delta(L,y)$.
The proof requires $\wedge$ to satisfy all four axioms (C1)–(C4).
The proof works for any two convergent sequences $x_n, y_n$ simultaneously.

explanations.text: The proof goal is stated this way.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $L = \lim x_n$... Want to show: $\exists N$ such that $|(x_n \wedge y) - (L \wedge y)| < \varepsilon$ for $n \geq N$

explanations.text: This is the key continuity step.
explanations.notes_quote: by (C3), $\exists \delta > 0$ such that $|(x_n \wedge y) - (L \wedge y)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $(x_n, y) \in B_\delta(L, y)$

explanations.text: $|x_n - L| < \delta$ places $(x_n,y)$ inside the disc $B_\delta(L,y)$ (the $y$-component doesn't change).
explanations.notes_quote: Since $x_n \to L$, $\exists N$ such that $|x_n - L| < \delta$ for $n \geq N$, which means $(x_n, y) \in B_\delta(L, y)$

explanations.text: Only (C3) (continuity) is needed for this proof. The other axioms are not invoked.
The proof as given only handles one variable sequence $x_n$ with fixed $y$. Extending to two sequences $x_n, y_n$ simultaneously requires a more general argument.

questions.text: For the chimera function $x \wedge y = \dfrac{xy}{\max(x, y, \frac{1}{2})}$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: When $x, y \leq \frac{1}{2}$: $\max(x,y,\frac{1}{2}) = \frac{1}{2}$, so $x \wedge y = 2xy$.
When $\frac{1}{2} < x \leq y$: $\max(x,y,\frac{1}{2}) = y$, so $x \wedge y = x = \min(x,y)$.
When $\frac{1}{2} < y \leq x$: $x \wedge y = y = \min(x,y)$.
The idempotent set is $A = \{0\} \cup [\frac{1}{2}, 1]$.
The idempotent set is $A = [0,1]$ (all elements are idempotent).

explanations.text: When both $x,y \leq 1/2$, the max is $1/2$, so $x \wedge y = xy/(1/2) = 2xy$.
explanations.notes_quote: If $x, y \leq \frac{1}{2}$: $\max(x, y, \frac{1}{2}) = \frac{1}{2}$, so $x \wedge y = 2xy$

explanations.text: When $1/2 < x \leq y$, $\max = y$, so $x \wedge y = xy/y = x = \min(x,y)$.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\frac{1}{2} < x \leq y$: $\max(x, y, \frac{1}{2}) = y$, so $x \wedge y = \frac{xy}{y} = x = \min(x,y)$

explanations.text: When $1/2 < y \leq x$, $\max = x$, so $x \wedge y = xy/x = y = \min(x,y)$.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\frac{1}{2} < y \leq x$: $\max(x, y, \frac{1}{2}) = x$, so $x \wedge y = \frac{xy}{x} = y = \min(x,y)$

explanations.text: For $z \geq 1/2$: $z \wedge z = z^2/z = z$. For $0 < z < 1/2$: $z \wedge z = 2z^2 \neq z$. For $z = 0$: $0 \wedge 0 = 0$.
explanations.notes_quote: $A = \{0\} \cup \left[\frac{1}{2}, 1\right]$

explanations.text: For $z \in (0,1/2)$: $z \wedge z = 2z^2 \neq z$, so these elements are NOT idempotent. $A \neq [0,1]$.

questions.source_ref: Section 6: Question 7.5 — Chimera Characterisation and Verification
questions.text: Regarding the chimera characterisation and verification of (C1)–(C4) for $x \wedge y = xy/\max(x,y,\frac{1}{2})$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: On $[0, \frac{1}{2}]$: $x \wedge y = 2xy$ is isomorphic to $F^2_\wedge$ via $\rho(z) = 2z$, since $\rho(x \wedge y) = 2(2xy) = 4xy = (2x)(2y) = \rho(x)\rho(y)$.
Outside $[0, \frac{1}{2}]$ (when $\max(x,y) > \frac{1}{2}$): $\wedge$ is exactly $\min(x,y) = F^1_\wedge$.
The Łukasiewicz t-norm $F^3_\wedge$ appears in this chimera.
(C3) verification: the boundary $x = 1/2$ or $y = 1/2$ poses no discontinuity because $2xy = \min(x,y)$ on that boundary.
(C4) for $x,y,z \leq 1/2$: $(x \wedge y) \wedge z = (2xy) \wedge z = 2(2xy)z = 4xyz = x \wedge (2yz) = 4xyz$.

explanations.text: The isomorphism $\rho(z) = 2z$ maps $[0,1/2]$ to $[0,1]$ and converts $2xy$ to $xy$ (product t-norm).
explanations.notes_quote: On $[0, \frac{1}{2}]$: $x \wedge y = 2xy$, which is isomorphic to the product t-norm $F^2_\wedge$ under the isomorphism $\rho(z) = 2z$, since $\rho(x \wedge y) = 2(2xy) = 4xy = (2x)(2y) = \rho(x)\rho(y)$

explanations.text: Outside the sub-interval, $\wedge = \min$.
explanations.notes_quote: Outside this interval (i.e. when $\max(x,y) > \frac{1}{2}$): $\wedge$ is exactly the minimum t-norm $F^1_\wedge = \min(x,y)$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state: 'The Łukasiewicz t-norm $F^3_\wedge$ does NOT appear in this particular chimera.'
explanations.notes_quote: The Łukasiewicz t-norm $F^3_\wedge$ does not appear in this particular chimera

explanations.text: On the boundary $y = x = 1/2$: $2 \cdot x \cdot 1/2 = x = \min(x,1/2)$; similarly for $y = 1/2$. So both formulas agree.
explanations.notes_quote: When $y \leq x = \frac{1}{2}$: $2xy = y = \min(x,y)$. When $x \leq y = \frac{1}{2}$: $2xy = x = \min(x,y)$. So both pieces agree on the boundary — no discontinuity

explanations.text: This is the associativity verification for the case $x,y,z \leq 1/2$.
explanations.notes_quote: $(x \wedge y) \wedge z = (2xy) \wedge z = 2(2xy)z = 4xyz$; $x \wedge (y \wedge z) = x \wedge (2yz) = 2x(2yz) = 4xyz$

questions.source_ref: Key Definitions Table — T-norm and T-conorm
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match terms to their definitions from the Key Definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A **t-norm** is a binary function $\wedge : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ satisfying (C1) Boolean, (C2) Increasing, (C3) Continuous, (C4) Associative.
A **t-conorm** is a binary function $\vee : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ satisfying (D1)–(D4), the dual axioms.
An **idempotent element** is a value $z \in [0,1]$ such that $z \wedge z = z$ (or $z \vee z = z$).
The **idempotent set** is $A = \{z \in [0,1] : z \wedge z \neq z\}$.
$B_\delta(a,b)$ is the open disc of radius $\delta$ centred at $(a,b)$: $\{(x,y) \in [0,1]^2 : (x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 < \delta^2\}$.

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: T-norm: A binary function $\wedge : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ satisfying (C1) Boolean, (C2) Increasing, (C3) Continuous, (C4) Associative

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: T-conorm: A binary function $\vee : [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ satisfying (D1)–(D4), the dual axioms

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Idempotent element: A value $z \in [0,1]$ such that $z \wedge z = z$ (or $z \vee z = z$)

explanations.text: The idempotent set is $A = \{z : z \wedge z = z\}$ (elements that ARE idempotent), not $\{z : z \wedge z \neq z\}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Idempotent set $A$: $A = \{z \in [0,1] : z \wedge z = z\}$

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: $B_\delta(a,b)$: Open disc of radius $\delta$ centred at $(a,b)$: $\{(x,y) \in [0,1]^2 : (x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 < \delta^2\}$

questions.source_ref: Key Definitions Table — Chimera, Types 2 and 3
questions.text: Which of the following correctly define 'chimera', 'Type 2 interval', and 'Type 3 interval'? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A **chimera** is a t-norm that behaves like $F^1_\wedge$ outside sub-intervals and like $F^2_\wedge$ or $F^3_\wedge$ on each sub-interval $[a_i,b_i]$ with $a_i,b_i \in A$.
A **Type 2 interval** $[a,b]$ has $a,b \in A$, $(a,b) \cap A = \varnothing$, and $z \wedge z \neq a$ for all $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^2_\wedge$.
A **Type 3 interval** $[a,b]$ has $a,b \in A$, $(a,b) \cap A = \varnothing$, and $z \wedge z = a$ for some $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^3_\wedge$.
A **Type 2 interval** is one where $z \wedge z = a$ for all $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^3_\wedge$.
$\wedge$-exponentiation is extended to real exponents via limits of rational exponents, using continuity (IVT + monotonicity).

explanations.text: Exact definition of chimera from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Chimera: A t-norm that behaves like $F^1_\wedge$ outside sub-intervals and like $F^2_\wedge$ or $F^3_\wedge$ on each sub-interval $[a_i, b_i]$ with $a_i, b_i \in A$

explanations.text: Exact definition of Type 2 interval.
explanations.notes_quote: Type 2 interval: Sub-interval $[a,b]$ with $a,b \in A$, $(a,b)\cap A = \varnothing$, and $z \wedge z \neq a$ for all $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^2_\wedge$

explanations.text: Exact definition of Type 3 interval.
explanations.notes_quote: Type 3 interval: Sub-interval $[a,b]$ with $a,b \in A$, $(a,b)\cap A = \varnothing$, and $z \wedge z = a$ for some $z \in (a,b)$ — isomorphic to $F^3_\wedge$

explanations.text: This is the definition of a Type 3 interval, not Type 2. Type 2 is defined by $z \wedge z \neq a$ for all interior $z$.
The key definitions table describes $\wedge$-exponentiation extended to real exponents via limits.
explanations.notes_quote: $\wedge$-exponentiation: $x^{\wedge n} = x \wedge \cdots \wedge x$ ($n$ times); extended to rationals via IVT and to reals via limits

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish order-preserving and order-reversing isomorphisms? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An **order-preserving isomorphism** $\rho : [a,b] \to [c,d]$ satisfies $\rho(x) \leq \rho(y) \iff x \leq y$.
An **order-reversing isomorphism** $\rho$ satisfies $\rho(x) \leq \rho(y) \iff x \geq y$.
In Lemma 6.4, the map $\rho(x) = c^{\wedge x}$ from $[0,2]$ to $[a,b]$ is order-reversing.
Composing two order-reversing isomorphisms gives an order-preserving isomorphism.
An order-reversing isomorphism cannot be used to establish that two structures are isomorphic.

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Order-preserving isomorphism: A bijection $\rho : [a,b] \to [c,d]$ with $\rho(x) \leq \rho(y) \iff x \leq y$ and $\rho(x \wedge y) = \rho(x) \star \rho(y)$

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Order-reversing isomorphism: A bijection $\rho$ with $\rho(x) \leq \rho(y) \iff x \geq y$ and the operation compatibility

explanations.text: The map $\rho(x) = c^{\wedge x}$ is order-reversing since larger exponents give smaller values (exponentiation is order-reversing).
explanations.notes_quote: This is an order-reversing isomorphism (since $\rho(x) < \rho(y)$ iff $x > y$)

explanations.text: The composition of two order-reversing maps is order-preserving. This is used in Lemma 6.4: $\rho \circ \sigma$ is order-preserving because both $\rho$ and $\sigma$ are order-reversing.
explanations.notes_quote: $\rho \circ \sigma : [0,1] \to [a,b]$ is an order-preserving isomorphism

explanations.text: Order-reversing isomorphisms do establish isomorphism between structures — but between structures with reversed orderings. In Lemma 6.4, an order-reversing map is used as an intermediate step.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly summarise key results of Week 7? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Any continuous t-norm satisfies the derived properties $x \wedge 0 = 0$, $x \wedge 1 = x$, and $x \wedge x \leq x$.
The Mostert–Shields theorem shows that any continuous t-norm is determined up to isomorphism by its idempotent set $A$.
The duality $(x \wedge y) = 1 - ((1-x) \vee (1-y))$ derives the t-conorm theorem from the t-norm theorem.
For the chimera $xy/\max(x,y,1/2)$, the idempotent set is $A = (0,1/2)$.
The isomorphism to $F^3_\wedge$ (type 3 intervals) is obtained by composing two order-reversing maps.

explanations.text: These are the three derived properties (i)–(iii) from (C1)–(C4).
explanations.notes_quote: (i) $x \wedge 0 = 0$; (ii) $x \wedge 1 = x$; (iii) $x \wedge x \leq x$

explanations.text: The idempotent set $A$ determines which sub-intervals exist and hence the full chimera structure.
explanations.notes_quote: LO4 (Mostert–Shields for $\wedge$): Any continuous t-norm is a chimera of $F^1_\wedge$, $F^2_\wedge$, $F^3_\wedge$. The idempotent set $A$ carves $[0,1]$ into sub-intervals

explanations.text: The duality formula converts any t-conorm into a t-norm, allowing the conjunction result to imply the disjunction result.
explanations.notes_quote: LO6 (Mostert–Shields for $\vee$): The dual theorem for t-conorms holds by the duality $(x \wedge y) = 1 - ((1-x) \vee (1-y))$

explanations.text: The idempotent set for $xy/\max(x,y,1/2)$ is $A = \{0\} \cup [1/2, 1]$, not $(0,1/2)$. Elements in $(0,1/2)$ are NOT idempotent.
explanations.notes_quote: $A = \{0\} \cup \left[\frac{1}{2}, 1\right]$

explanations.text: The isomorphism to $F^3_\wedge$ is $\rho \circ \sigma$ where $\rho$ (order-reversing) and $\sigma(x) = 2(1-x)$ (order-reversing) compose to give an order-preserving isomorphism.
explanations.notes_quote: LO5 (Isomorphisms): the isomorphism to $F^3_\wedge$ requires composing an order-reversing isomorphism with another order-reversing map $\sigma(x) = 2(1-x)$
